items.q: Is background radiation always present, even when no source is being studied?
items.correct: Yes — natural sources (rocks, cosmic rays, food) produce a low level of radiation everywhere on Earth.
items.misconception: No — background radiation only appears near nuclear power stations.
items.random_wrong: Yes, but only in cities with old buildings.
items.why: Students associate radiation exclusively with nuclear technology and don't realise natural background dose is ubiquitous.

items.q: Why is gravitational potential energy near a planet treated as negative?
items.correct: Because the zero is defined at infinity; objects bound to the planet have lower (negative) potential energy than at infinity.
items.misconception: Gravitational potential energy is negative because gravity always points downwards.
items.random_wrong: Gravitational potential energy is negative because gravity does negative work.
items.why: Students confuse the negative sign as 'direction' rather than understanding it as a consequence of choosing infinity as the zero point.

items.q: What is required to induce an emf in a coil using a magnet?
items.correct: A changing magnetic flux linkage through the coil — the magnet (or coil) must be moving or the field must be changing.
items.misconception: Just having a magnet near the coil induces an emf, even when both are stationary.
items.random_wrong: A current in the coil is what induces an emf in the magnet.
items.why: Students remember 'magnet + coil = electricity' but miss that the field must be changing for an emf to be induced (Faraday's law).

items.q: Which nuclide has the highest BE per nucleon?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Fe-56. “H-1” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's 931 MeV/c². “100 MeV/c²” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If mass defect is 0.1 u, energy in MeV (1 u = 931.5 MeV):
items.why: The real figure is 93.2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
